Gordon Boyd3, Andrew G Day4, Micheal C Wallace21Queen's University, Kingston, Canada. 2Department of Neurosurgery, Kingston General Hospital, Kingston, Canada. 3Departmentof Medicine (Neurology) and Critical Care, Kingston General Hospital, Kingston, Canada. 4Clinical Research Centre, Kingston GeneralHospital, Kingston, CanadaContact address: Imelda M Galvin, Queen's University, Kingston, ON, Canada. Editorial group: Cochrane Anaesthesia Group.Publication status and date: New search for studies and content updated (no change to conclusions), published in Issue 1, 2015.

Citation:  Galvin IM, Levy R, Boyd JG, Day AG, Wallace MC. Cooling for cerebral protection during brain surgery. Cochrane Database ofSystematic Reviews 2015, Issue 1. Art. No.: CD006638. DOI: 10.1002/14651858.CD006638.pub3.Copyright (c) 2015 The Cochrane Collaboration. Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.A B S T R A C TBackgroundPatients undergoing neurosurgery are at risk of cerebral ischaemia with resultant cerebral hypoxia and neuronal cell death. This canincrease both the risk of mortality and long term neurological disability. Induced hypothermia has been shown to reduce the risk ofcerebral ischaemic damage in both animal studies and in humans who have been resuscitated following cardiac arrest. This had lead to anincreasing interest in its neuroprotective potential in neurosurgical patients. This review was originally published in 2011 and did not findany evidence of either efectiveness or harm in these patients. This updated review was designed to capture current evidence to readdressthese issues.ObjectivesTo evaluate the efectiveness and safety profile of induced hypothermia versus normothermia for neuroprotection in patients undergoingbrain surgery. Efectiveness was to be measured in terms of short and long term mortality and functional neurological outcomes. Safetywas to be assessed in terms of the rate of the adverse events infection, myocardial infarction, ischaemic stroke, congestive cardiac failureand any other adverse events reported by the authors of the included studies.For the original review, the authors searched the databases Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL), MEDLINE (OvidSP),EMBASE (OvidSP) and LILACS to November 2010. For the updated review all these databases were re-searched from November 2010 toMay 2014.

For both the original and updated versions, grey literature was sought by searching reference lists of identified studies and relevant reviewarticles, and conference proceedings. No language restrictions were applied.Selection criteriaAs in the original review, we included randomized controlled trials (RCTs) of induced hypothermia versus normothermia forneuroprotection in patients of any age and gender undergoing brain surgery, which addressed mortality, neurological morbidity or adverseevent outcomes.Three review authors independently extracted data and two independently assessed the risk of bias of the included studies. Anydiscrepancies were resolved by discussion between authors.Cooling for cerebral protection during brain surgery (Review)Copyright (c) 2015 The Cochrane Collaboration. Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.CochraneLibraryTrusted evidence.Informed decisions.Better health.

Cochrane Database of Systematic ReviewsMain resultsIn this updated review, one new ongoing study was found but no new eligible completed studies were identified. This update was thereforeconducted using the same four studies included in the original review. These studies included a total of 1219 participants, mean age 40 to54 years. All included studies were reported as RCTs. Two were multicentred, together including a total of 1114 patients who underwentcerebral aneurysm clipping, and were judged to have an overall low risk of bias. The other two studies were single centred. One included80 patients who had a craniotomy following severe traumatic brain injury and was judged to have an unclear or low risk of bias. The otherstudy included 25 patients who underwent hemicranicectomy to relieve oedema following cerebral infarction and was judged to have anunclear or high risk of bias. All studies assessed hypothermia versus normothermia. Overall 608 participants received hypothermia withtarget temperatures ranging from 32.5 degC to 35 degC, and 611 were assigned to normothermia with the actual temperatures recorded in thisgroup ranging form 36.5 degC to 38 degC. For those who were cooled, 556 had cooling commenced immediately afer induction of anaesthesiathat was continued until the surgical objective of aneurysm clipping was achieved, and 52 had cooling commenced immediately afersurgery and continued for 48 to 96 hours.

Pooled estimates of efect were calculated for the outcomes mortality during treatment or follow-up (ranging from in-hospital to one year);neurological outcome measured in terms of the Glasgow Outcome Score (GOS) of 3 or less; and adverse events of infections, myocardialinfarction, ischaemic stroke and congestive cardiac failure. With regards to mortality, the risk of dying if allocated to hypothermia comparedto normothermia was not statistically significantly diferent (risk ratio (RR) 0.87, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.59 to 1.27, P = 0.47). Therewas no indication that the time at which cooling was started afected the risk of dying (RR with intraoperative cooling 0.95, 95% CI 0.60 to1.51, P = 0.83; RR for cooling postoperatively 0.67, 95% CI 0.34 to 1.35, P = 0.26). For the neurological outcome, the risk of having a pooroutcome with a GOS of 3 or less was not statistically diferent in those who received hypothermia versus normothermia (RR 0.80, 95% CI0.61 to 1.04, P = 0.09). Again there was no indication that the time at which cooling was started afected this result. Regarding adverseevents, there was no statistically significant diference in the incidence in those allocated to hypothermia versus normothermia for risk ofsurgical infection (RR 1.20, 95% CI 0.73 to 1.97, P = 0.48), myocardial infarction (RR 1.86, 95% CI 0.69 to 4.98, P = 0.22), ischaemic stroke (RR0.93, 95% CI 0.82 to 1.05, P = 0.24) or congestive heart failure (RR 0.85, 95% CI 0.60 to 1.21, P = 0.38). In contrast to other outcomes, wheretime of application of cooling did not change the statistical significance of the efect estimates, there was a weak statistically significantincreased risk of infection in those who were cooled postoperatively versus those who were not cooled (RR 1.77, 95% CI 1.05 to 2.98, P =0.03). Overall, as in the original review, no evidence was found that the use of induced hypothermia was either beneficial or harmful inpatients undergoing neurosurgery.Authors' conclusions

We found no evidence that the use of induced hypothermia was associated with a significant reduction in mortality or severe neurologicaldisability, or an increase in harm in patients undergoing neurosurgery.P L A I N   L A N G U A G E   S U M M A R YNo evidence that cooling patients undergoing brain surgery helps to either reduce death and severe disability or increase harmBrain surgery is ofen required in patients who have abnormal brain blood vessels, had a stroke, sustained a traumatic injury or havesufered spontaneous bleeding into the brain. While surgery can be lifesaving it can also cause further damage due to an associatedinterruption and reduction of blood flow to the brain cells. Without an adequate supply of blood the brain cells are deprived of oxygenand they may die as a result. If brain cells are lost in large numbers or in important areas of the brain, the patient may die or be severelydisabled. This has led to a search for ways to protect the brain cells during brain operations. Studies in animals have shown that loweringthe body temperature can help to protect the brain cells in circumstances when the blood supply to the cells is compromised. Similarly,studies in humans who have been resuscitated afer their heart stopped beating have shown that lowering their body temperature helpsto reduce brain damage. The purpose of this updated version of a previous review of the same title was to determine whether coolingpatients who were having brain surgery reduced death and serious disability, or was associated with increased risk of harm. A detailedsearch of the available literature up until May 2014 identified four eligible studies that included a total of 1219 participants. Their resultswere combined to answer these questions. No evidence was found that cooling patients who are having brain operations reduced the riskof death or severe disability, or produced significant harm.

B A C K G R O U N DDescription of the conditionWhen cerebral blood flow is disrupted due to a traumatic,haemorrhagic or ischaemic insult, anoxic cell membranedepolarization occurs. Neuronal depolarization opens calcium andsodium channels to allow these cations into the cells (Rothman1995). The resultant osmotic gradient pulls chloride and water intothe cells causing cellular swelling and breakdown. The hypoxiccerebral cells also release glutamate, leading to an increase inintracellular calcium levels and further cell damage (Choi 1990).As part of a generalized and localized inflammatory responseoxygen free radicals are produced, which cause mitochondrialdysfunction and further neuronal cell necrosis and apoptosis. Whenthe cerebral blood flow is restored further damage can occur due toa secondary superoxide surge, increased production of nitric oxideand disruption of the blood brain barrier (Wei 1999). This phaseis termed reperfusion injury and can lead to even more severedamage than the initial hypoxic insult.Patients who have sustained traumatic brain injury, intracerebralhaemorrhage or ischaemia ofen require surgical intervention,as do those who require repair of vascular malformations.Surgery carries the potential for further cerebral ischaemiadue to interruption of cerebral perfusion, vasospasm, impairedautoregulation and hyperaemic reperfusion injury, furthercompromising clinical outcomes (Leslie 2003). This has lead to asearch for neuroprotective strategies to limit the extent of surgicalinduced damaged (El Beheiry 2012).

Description of the interventionThe use of hypothermia in neurosurgery was first described inthe 1950s, with profound hypothermia being postulated as havingneuroprotective efects (Hendrick 1959; Lougheed 1955). Howeverit was largely abandoned due to excessive complications and pooroutcomes (Kwak 1972; Michenfelder 1968; Steen 1980). There was aresurgence of interest in this area in the 1990s with studies showingthat mild hypothermia improved outcomes in animal models ofischaemic and traumatic brain injury (Clifon 1991; Goto 1993;Karibe 1994). Clinical studies in post-cardiac arrest populationsshowed improved outcomes, triggering further interest in itspotential neuroprotective efects in ischaemic stroke, traumaticbrain injury, cerebral aneurysm surgery etc (Els 2006; Holzer 2002;Qiu 2007; Todd 2005). Common to all of these conditions is thedisruption of cerebral blood flow with resultant ischaemic celldeath.How the intervention might workHypothermia protects the brain during hypoxic injury by areduction in cerebral metabolism by approximately 5% for everydegree reduction in temperature, attenuating or delaying anoxicdepolarization, and reducing glutamate release (Laptook 1995).It also reduces the production of excitatory amino acids andsuppresses nitric oxide and superoxide production (Drury 2014).In vitro studies have shown that mild hypothermia reducesneuronal apoptosis by reducing the production and activation ofhypoxia associated proteins that mediate cell death (Xu 2002).Animal studies have shown that hypothermia also attenuates themechanisms involved in reperfusion injury (Thoresen 1997).

Why it is important to do this reviewRecently there has been increasing focus on the role of hypothermiain those people undergoing neurosurgical procedures includinganeurysm repair, haematoma evacuation, and decompressivecraniectomy (Els 2006; Hindman 1999; Qiu 2007). The originalreview (Milani 2011) of the value of hypothermia in neurosurgicalpopulations was carried out at the beginning of this emergingfocus of research. It aimed to provide an insight into the overallbenefits among and across neurosurgical populations, provideevidence based guidance on the optimal temperature targets andthe timing and duration of treatment as well as its safety profile.That review incorporated existing evidence up until November2010. This updated review was conducted to determine what thecurrent state of evidence is in this regard.O B J E C T I V E STo evaluate the efectiveness and safety profile of inducedhypothermia versus normothermia for neuroprotection in patientsundergoing brain surgery. Efectiveness was to be measured interms of short and long term mortality and functional neurologicaloutcomes. Safety was to be assessed in terms of the rate of theadverse events infection, myocardial infarction, ischaemic stroke,congestive cardiac failure and any other adverse events reported bythe authors of the included studies.M E T H O D SCriteria for considering studies for this reviewTypes of studiesSimilar to the original review on this topic (Milani 2011), weintended to include both randomized and quasi-randomizedcontrolled trials.

Quasi-randomized trials were defined as trials using a method ofallocation of participants to diferent forms of care that was nottruly random (for example allocation by date of birth, day of theweek, medical record number, month of the year, or the order inwhich participants were included in the study).Types of participantsAny patient undergoing brain surgery (vascular, tumour, braininjury, intracranial arteriovenous malformations, etc), without ageor gender restrictions, who were participants in a trial of cooling atany time in the perioperative period, that is either preoperatively,intraoperatively or postoperatively.Types of interventionsNeurosurgical procedures where a deliberate strategy to cool thebrain in the perioperative period was compared to no specificattempt to manipulate the brain temperature. While there isno generally accepted classification for levels of hypothermia,we defined the target levels of hypothermia as follows (wheretemperatures refer to brain or core values).*Mild: down to 32 oC.*Moderate: from 32 oC to 28 oC.*Deep: under 28 oC.Cooling for cerebral protection during brain surgery (Review)Copyright (c) 2015 The Cochrane Collaboration. Cochrane Database of Systematic ReviewsTypes of outcome measuresStudies were eligible for inclusion if they reported any of thefollowing outcome measures.1. Mortality rate during treatment or the follow-up period.2. Glasgow Outcome Score (GOS) of 3 or below. This cut of pointwas chosen as it marks the transition point between recovery toindependent or dependent living and worse outcomes requiringhigh level care. Those with a GOS of 4 or more recover normalor near normal independence with employment possible witheither no or some special provisions, while a GOS of 3 or lesssignifies severe disability, partial or total loss of independenceor even death (Jennett 1975) (Appendix 1).The following adverse events were reported on.1. Any surgical infective complications including cerebralinfection, septicaemia, pulmonary infection etc.2. Myocardial infarction, defined as a raised cardiac troponinlevel accompanied by at least one of the following: ischaemicsymptoms, development of pathological Q waves on theelectrocardiogram (ECG), ECG changes indicating ischaemia (STsegment elevation or depression) (JESC/ACC Committee 2000).3. Ischaemic stroke, defined as a persistent focal neurologicaldeficit of presumed ischaemic origin lasting more than 24 hoursand confirmed by computed tomography (CT) or magneticresonance imaging (MRI) (Goldstein 1994).4. Congestive heart failure, defined as clinical signs of myocardialfailure and requiring medical treatment.5. Intracranial haemorrhage, defined as bleeding into the braincaused by the rupture of cerebral blood vessels (includingextradural or subdural haemorrhage) confirmed by CT or MRI.6. Any other adverse events which were reported by the authors ofthe primary studies.

Electronic searchesFor the original review (Milani 2011), the authors searched theCochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL) in TheCochrane Library (2010, Issue 10); MEDLINE (OvidSP) (1950 toNovember 2010); EMBASE (OvidSP) (1980 to November 2010);LILACS (1982 to November 2010) and CINAHL (1982 to November2010).For the updated review we searched all of the above databasesfrom November 2010 to May 2014. The same search terms used inthe original review were used in the updated version (CENTRAL,Appendix 2; MEDLINE, Appendix 3; EMBASE, Appendix 4; LILACS,Appendix 5; CINAHL, Appendix 6).Searching other resourcesFor both the original and updated versions, grey literature wassought by searching reference lists of identified studies andrelevant review articles, and conference proceedings. No languagerestrictions were applied.Data collection and analysisSelection of studiesFor this updated version, three authors (IMG, RL, JGB)independently reviewed the articles retrieved by the updatedsearch from November 2010 to May 2014 to identify studies thatmay have been eligible for inclusion. One review author (IMG)was responsible for contacting primary study authors shouldadditional details have been required for determination of studyeligibility and the quality. For the original version, two of theoriginal authors scanned records retrieved by the initial search toidentify potentially eligible studies and three of the original authorsindependently retrieved and reviewed all full-text articles (Milani2011).

Data extraction and managementFor this updated version a new data extraction form was designedby one author (IMG) (Appendix 7). The form was designed toextract data from any new studies which may be identified aswell as to re-extract data from the studies included in the originalreview. This approach was taken to provide the reader withmore comprehensive information about study methods, allow amore detailed assessment of risk of bias and ensure consistencyof data extraction for all eligible studies. Three authors (IMG,RL, JGB) independently extracted the data using this form. Allitems recommended in Section 7 of the Cochrane Handbook forSystematic Reviews of Interventions (Higgins 2011) were addressedin the data extraction process. Extracted data were entered initiallyonto the data extraction form and then into the correspondingfields in the Review Manager sofware (RevMan 5.2).Assessment of risk of bias in included studiesThe risk of bias of the included studies was assessed independentlyby two review authors (IMG, RL) using the Cochrane Collaborationtool for assessing risk of bias, as described in Section 8 of theCochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions (Higgins2011). Any discrepancies were resolved by discussion with a thirdreview author (JGB). For each included primary study, bias wasassessed in seven domains:

1. random sequence generation,2. allocation concealment,3. performance bias,4. detection bias,5. attrition bias,6. reporting bias, and7. other bias.For each domain, we determined the risk of bias as 'low', 'unclear'or 'high' according to the methods used in the study to ensure theminimization of each form of bias. In general these ratings wereapplied as follows.*The low risk rating was given where information was availablethat clearly demonstrated that eforts were made to ensureminimal bias in that domain and the described methods wererobust enough to have a high likelihood of being efective.*The unclear risk rating was given when the information that wasavailable was insuficient to be confident that the method usedto minimize bias was robust enough to be efective.Cooling for cerebral protection during brain surgery (Review)Copyright (c) 2015 The Cochrane Collaboration. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews*The high risk rating was given when the study did not report anymethod to minimize bias in that domain.Measures of treatment efectCategorical outcomes were presented as risk ratios (RR) with 95%confidence intervals (CI). No continuous or time to event outcomemeasures were addressed as part of this review.Unit of analysis issuesData were analysed using participants who were individuallyrandomized to each intervention group for each outcomeaddressed.Dealing with missing dataFor each included trial (where such information was available) theproportion of participants who were missing from the final analysiswas recorded. An intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis was used, wherepossible.Assessment of heterogeneity

Clinical heterogeneity was assessed by comparing studyparticipants, settings, inventions and ancillary treatments.Methodologic heterogeneity was assessed by comparing the risk ofbias in the included studies. Where included studies were deemedto be adequately homogeneous in these respects, statisticalheterogeneity was assessed using the Chi2 test and the I2 statistic.A P value less than 0.10 and I2 value in excess of 50% were taken asindicative of important heterogeneity (Higgins 2003).Assessment of reporting biasesTo determine the presence or absence of reporting bias, we plannedto examine funnel plots for each meta-analysis that included 10or more studies to determine if they were symmetrical. As onlyfour studies were eligible for inclusion in this updated review anaccurate assessment of reporting bias was not possible on thisoccasion but is planned for future updates if adequate numbers (10or more) of eligible studies become available.Data synthesisMeta-analysis was conducted by the authors of the original review.RevMan (RevMan 5.2) was used to perform the analyses for eachoutcome in the present review. For each outcome, all eligiblestudies that reported the outcome were included and participantswho were treated with hypothermia were compared with thosewho were in the normothermia group. A fixed-efect model wasused in the absence of important heterogeneity and a random-efects model if heterogeneity was deemed likely (DerSimonian1986).Subgroup analysis and investigation of heterogeneityShould adequate numbers of eligible studies (at least two foreach subgroup) have been identified, we planned to conduct thefollowing subgroup analyses.

*Outcomes by diferent types of participants, such as ischaemicstoke; haemorrhagic stoke; aneurysmal subarachnoidhaemorrhage; epilepsy etc.*Outcomes by diferent age groups, i.e. children versus adults.*Outcomes by diferent cooling periods, i.e. preoperatively,intraoperatively and postoperatively.Due to the small number of eligible studies retrieved, only onesubgroup analysis was performed comparing outcomes in termsof mortality, GOS, surgical infection and myocardial infarction forthose cooled during surgery versus those cooled afer surgery. Theremaining subgroup analyses are still planned for future updatesof this review, depending on availability of adequate numbers (atleast two for each subgroup) of eligible studies.Sensitivity analysisTwo sensitivity analyses were performed as follows.*Analysis excluding those studies deemed to have a lowmethodologic quality as evidenced by a high risk of bias.*Analysis assuming two opposite scenarios for missing data. Inthe 'best case' scenario all missing patients in the hypothermiagroup were assumed to have a good outcome while all those inthe normothermic group were assumed to have bad recovery. Inthe 'worst case' scenario the reverse assumption was made.R E S U L T SDescription of studiesResults of the search

A total of 7272 citations were identified (5870 by the original search(Milani 2011) and 1402 by the updated search). Of these 7272citations, a total of 7014 were excluded on initial screening as itwas obvious from the titles or abstracts that they were ineligible. Atotal of 258 abstracts were examined further for eligibility. Of these,one new ongoing study was identified (Nuegebauer 2013), 253 wereexcluded, and four were included in this review (Figure 1). *Els (Els 2006) enrolled patients undergoing hemicraniectomy totreat oedema afer malignant supratentorial infarction of morethan two thirds of one cerebral hemisphere.*Hindman and Todd (Hindman 1999; Todd 2005) enrolledpatients undergoing cerebral aneurysm clipping.*Qiu (Qiu 2007) enrolled patients undergoing unilateralcraniotomy for operative evacuation of intracranial haematomaor massive cerebral contusion, or both, in patients with non-penetrating traumatic brain injury.DesignAll the included studies were reported to be randomized controlledtrials. All studies with the exception of Els (Els 2006) reported therandomization process used.Two studies (Hindman 1999; Todd 2005) were multicentre studies.Hindman (Hindman 1999) was performed in five academic medicalcentres and Todd (Todd 2005) enrolled participants from 30 centres.The other two studies (Els 2006; Qiu 2007) were conducted at singleuniversity centres.

These trials included a total of 1219 participants with the followingcharacteristics.Hindman (Hindman 1999) included 114 participants (9.4% of thetotal), 52 participants with acute subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH)and 62 without SAH. The mean age was 51 years in the hypothermicgroup and 54 years in the normothermic group; 38 participantswere men and 76 were women.Todd (Todd 2005) included 1000 patients (82% of the total) whohad SAH no more than 14 days before planned surgical aneurysmclipping. The median age was 52 years in the hypothermic groupand 53 years in the normothermic group; 344 participants were menand 655 were women.All of the participants in the studies by Hindman and Todd(Hindman 1999; Todd 2005) had a preoperative World Federation ofNeurological Surgeons (WFNS) score (Teasdale 1988) (Appendix 8)of I, II or III. This clinical grading system, proposed by the WFNS, isbased on the Glasgow Coma Score (Teasdale 1974) and the findingof a motor deficit in patients with SAH. Patients classified as gradeI have the best prognosis; those with grade II have a fair prognosis;and patients with grade III tend to have poor recovery. Patients withgrade IV have poor prognosis and those with grade V are classifiedas moribund patients.

Els (Els 2006) included 25 severely ill participants (2.1% of thetotal) operated on following a supratentorial ischaemic event withinfarction of cerebral tissue. The mean age was 48.9 years; 15participants were men and 10 were women.Qiu (Qiu 2007) assessed 80 participants (6.6% of the total) whohad non-penetrating severe traumatic brain injury (TBI). The meanage was 41.3 years in the hypothermic group and 40.2 years inthe normothermic group; 52 participants were men and 28 werewomen.All four included studies (Els 2006; Hindman 1999; Qiu 2007;Todd2005) assessed hypothermia versus normothermia for brainsurgery.Hindman (Hindman 1999) induced hypothermia afer the startof anaesthesia and the positioning of the patient, with a targettemperature of 33.5 oC. The initial operating room temperature was20 oC to 22.0 oC. Participants in the hypothermic group were cooledby a forced-air cooling and heating blanket set to deliver unwarmedair (room temperature); a cooling and heating mattress connectedto a heating-cooling unit set to 20.0 degC; and intravenous fluidsand anaesthetic gases that were not warmed until the clip wasapplied. Participants in the normothermic group were maintainedat a target oesophageal temperature of 36.5 oC by a cooling andheating mattress set to 38.0 oC; a forced-air cooling and heatingblanket set to 36.0 oC to 38.0 oC; and intravenous fluids and inspiredanaesthetic gases warmed to 37.0 oC. At the time of clip application,patients assigned to the hypothermia group were significantlycolder than those assigned to the normothermia group, with amedian temperature of 33.7 oC versus 36.6 degC, respectively (P <0.0001).

Todd (Todd 2005) used a forced-air blanket connected to aheating-cooling unit to control the temperature for all patients.Hypothermia was induced afer the start of anaesthesia andpositioning of the patient, with a target temperature of 32.5 degCto 33.5 oC. The time from induction of anaesthesia to first clipplacement was 217 +- 101 minutes and the temperature was 33.3+- 0.8 oC. Rewarming of patients began afer the last aneurysm cliphad been secured and the temperature two hours afer surgerywas 36.4 +- 1.0 oC. The temperature of the normothermic groupwas kept between 36.0 oC and 37 degC and the time from inductionof anaesthesia to the first clip placement was 214 +- 82 minutes.Both groups, normothermic and hypothermic, otherwise receivedsimilar treatment.Els (Els 2006) induced hypothermia immediately afer the operationwith a target temperature of 35.0 oC. In the hypothermic group10 participants were cooled by an intravenous cooling device;two participants were cooled by an external cooling device incombination with cool ventilator air fanning the body surface.All participants were cooled for 48 hours and active rewarmingat a rate of 1 oC per 24 hours was applied. The normothermicgroup received the same treatment as the hypothermic group,although details were not provided regarding how the equality ofancillary treatment was ensured. The authors did not report howthe temperature was maintained in the normothermic group.

Qiu (Qiu 2007) induced hypothermia for four days immediately aferthe operation, at a temperature of 33.0 oC to 35.0 oC, with the use ofa water-circulating cooling blanket and, if necessary, a cooling caparound the head with circulating water at a temperature about 4oC. The target temperature was generally achieved within 2.5 hours.For those who failed to achieve the target temperature within twohours refrigerated ice bags were placed around the neck. Patientswere passively rewarmed over 10 to 24 hours. The normothermicgroup received the same treatment as the hypothermic group inall other ways, although again details were lacking as to how thiswas ensured. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews AllocationThree of the included studies (Hindman 1999; Qiu 2007; Todd 2005)provided details of the randomization process used. Methods usedvaried in rigour from a simple randomization table to permutedblock randomization but, since a reasonable method was used in allthree studies, they were each judged to have a low risk of selectionbias due to inadequate randomization. One study (Els 2006) statedthat the participants were randomly assigned to the interventionsbut provided no details of the method used and was thereforejudged to be at unclear risk of bias in this domain.Regarding allocation concealment, two studies described robustmethods (Hindman 1999; Todd 2005), the third (Qiu 2007)mentioned that allocation concealment was used but did notdescribe a method. and the fourth (Els 2006) did not mention itsuse. The risk of bias in this domain was therefore judged to be lowin the studies by Hindman and Todd (Hindman 1999; Todd 2005),unclear in the study by Qiu (Qiu 2007), and high in the study by Els(Els 2006).Two studies (Hindman 1999; Todd 2005) provided clear detailsregarding blinding of study personnel and outcome assessorsand were assigned a low risk of bias in these domains. In thestudy by Qiu (Qiu 2007) the study personnel were unaware oftreatment assignments but it was not clear if the outcome assessorswere blinded. This may have had implications for more subjectiveoutcomes including neurologic recovery. However, as the GOSclearly defined the scoring criteria it was unlikely that the lack ofblinding of outcome assessors resulted in significant bias, thereforethe risk was judged to be low.

The study by Els (Els 2006) did not describe any method of blindingeither study personnel or outcome assessors. For the reasonsdetailed above, it was assigned a low risk of bias due to notblinding the outcome assessors. However, as lack of blinding ofstudy personnel lef the potential for intentional or unintentionaldiferences in ancillary treatment it was assigned a high risk of biasin this domain.In the studies by Hindman and Todd (Hindman 1999; Todd 2005)the numbers lost to follow-up were relatively small, three andone respectively. These studies were therefore judged to be at alow risk of attrition bias. Information was not available regardingcompleteness of follow-up in the studies by Els and Qiu (Els 2006;Qiu 2007) and therefore they were both judged to be at unclear riskof attrition bias.Two studies reported outcomes as pre-specified (Hindman 1999;Todd 2005) while the other two studies (Els 2006; Qiu 2007) didnot pre-specify their outcome measures. Risk of reporting bias wastherefore judged to be unclear in the latter two studies (Els 2006;Qiu 2007).Other potential sources of biasIn the study by Qiu (Qiu 2007), the normothermic group had braintemperatures between 36.5 oC and 37.5 oC with rectal temperatureeven higher at 37.5 oC to 38 oC. Some of the normothermicgroup were therefore hyperthermic, which may have worsenedtheir outcome making the intervention (hypothermia) seem moreefective than it really was.Efects of interventionsSee: Summary of findings for the main comparison Cooling forcerebral protection during brain surgery

Any other adverse events reportedNone of the trials in this review reported any adverse efects directlyrelated to the therapy other than those reported above.D I S C U S S I O NSummary of main resultsThe efect estimates for the main outcomes are detailed in thesummary of findings table (Summary of findings for the maincomparison). Among patients undergoing craniotomy for variousneurosurgical indications including aneurysm clipping, traumaticbrain injury and Ischaemic stroke:*there is no evidence that intraoperative or postoperativehypothermia significantly reduces or increases mortality;*there is no evidence that intraoperative or postoperativehypothermia significant alters the risk of severe neurologicdisability;*there is no evidence that intraoperative hypothermiasignificantly alters the risk of intracranial haemorrhage,ischaemic stroke or congestive cardiac failure;*there is no evidence that intraoperative or postoperativehypothermia significantly alters the risk of myocardialinfarction;*there is some weak evidence that postoperative hypothermiamay increase the risk of infective complications.Overall completeness and applicability of evidenceThe findings of this review need to be interpreted in light of thefollowing limitations.

Firstly, there is an overall lack of eligible high quality randomizedcontrolled trials. The original search and this updated searchidentified only four eligible studies and of these only two (Hindman1999; Todd 2005) were judged to have an overall low risk of bias. Thelack of adequate evidence limited the accuracy and reliability of theresults. For the same reason, the scope of the review was limitedand it was not unable to fulfil its objectives in terms of analysing theefectiveness and safely of hypothermia by age group or underlyingneurosurgical condition.Secondly, there was potentially important clinical heterogeneityin terms of the underlying neurosurgical condition in the foureligible studies. While all patients in both the Hindman and Toddstudies (Hindman 1999; Todd 2005) had surgery for aneurysmclipping, the study by Hindman (Hindman 1999) included boththose who had and had not had a subarachnoid haemorrhage whilethe study by Todd (Todd 2005) was confined to those who hada confirmed subarachnoid haemorrhage. However, participantswere homogenous in terms of premorbid neurologic disability,all being required to have a premorbid Modified Rankin scoreof 0 or 1 (Appendix 9), and patients in both trials who hadhad a subarachnoid haemorrhage were required to have a WorldFederation of Neurological Surgeons score of I, II or III (Appendix8). The participants in the other two studies were more diverseas Els (Els 2006) studied those patients having decompressivecraniectomy for cerebral oedema afer acute ischaemic stoke andQiu (Qiu 2007) studied those who had undergone craniotomyfollowing traumatic brain injury. Among these two studies (Els2006; Qiu 2007) there were diferences in both the duration ofcooling and ancillary treatment, and either of these may havecontributed to the diversity and heterogeneity. Pooled estimates ofefect that included those studies (Els 2006; Qiu 2007) in particularshould therefore be interpreted with this limitation in mind.

Thirdly, over 90% of the participants underwent surgery foraneurysm clipping (Hindman 1999; Todd 2005), limiting theapplicability of these results to other neurosurgical populations.The results of this review should be viewed at best as a generalindication of the efectiveness and safety of intraoperative andpostoperative hypothermia in patients undergoing craniotomy,with the important caveat that no robust or reliable conclusions canbe drawn from it regarding the efects of hypothermia in specificneurosurgical populations aside perhaps from those undergoingcerebral aneurysm clipping.Quality of the evidenceThe quality of the evidence contributing to the main outcomes isdetailed in the summary of findings table (Summary of findings forthe main comparison).The overall quality of the evidence contributing to the efectestimates for mortality and the Glasgow Outcome Score wasdeemed to be moderate, with data coming from two studies with anoverall low risk of bias, a third with a low or unclear risk of bias (Qiu2007) and the fourth having a high or unclear risk of bias (Els 2006).For myocardial infarction, the quality of the evidence was rated asmoderate as one of the studies had a high or unclear risk of bias (Els2006).The quality of the evidence contributing to efect estimates forischaemic stoke and congestive heart failure was deemed to be highas both studies reporting these outcomes had a low risk of bias(Hindman 1999; Todd 2005).For the estimate of infective complications, the quality of theevidence was judged to be low as there was significant statisticalheterogeneity between studies and we lacked information on howinfective complications were defined and diagnosed. The numbersanalysed by individual studies for each infective complication werealso small and the confidence intervals were not reported.Cooling for cerebral protection during brain surgery (Review)Copyright (c) 2015 The Cochrane Collaboration. Cochrane Database of Systematic ReviewsPotential biases in the review processAn unrestricted comprehensive literature search was conductedfor both the original review and the updated review with theaim of identifying all studies eligible for inclusion, and so tominimize reporting and selection bias. The small number ofeligible studies that were identified, however, means that theefect estimates should not be taken as an accurate indication ofthe true efectiveness and safely of induced hypothermia acrossneurosurgical populations.Agreements and disagreements with other studies orreviewsTwo other recent systematic reviews addressing the efectivenessand safety of perioperative hypothermia in patients undergoingsurgery for intracranial aneurysms found similar results (Li 2012;Zhao 2012). A Cochrane review (Li 2012) of mild intraoperativehypothermia in patients undergoing surgery for both ruptured andunruptured intracranial aneurysms did not show any statisticallysignificant diference in outcomes for those treated withhypothermia versus normothermia. A second systematic review(Zhao 2012) addressing the use of intraoperative hypothermiain patients undergoing intracranial aneurysm surgery similarlydid not show any significant diferences in mortality, neurologicoutcome or adverse events between the groups. Like this review,both were limited by the lack of availability of eligible studies andboth concluded that currently there was insuficient evidence tosupport the routine use of hypothermia in this population.

We found only one eligible study (Qiu 2007) addressing the role ofhypothermia in patients undergoing surgery following traumaticbrain injury, and it is important to note that. That study had anumber of methodological problems limiting the ability of thisreview to determine the role of hypothermia in this population.A post hoc analysis (Clifon 2012) of two randomized controlledtrials of hypothermia for severe traumatic brain injury looked atthe efect of hypothermia in those patients who had haematomassurgically evacuated within 24 hours of injury. They found thatinduction of hypothermia to 35 degC before or soon afer craniotomyand maintenance at 33 degC for 48 hours postoperatively wasassociated with an improved neurologic outcome at six months.They postulated that early induction of hypothermia may bebeneficial in those requiring surgical evacuation of post-traumaticintracranial haematoma and emphasize the need for future trials inthis area that target similar temperature goals.There is a paucity of trials addressing the value of hypothermiain all neurosurgical populations, especially in those undergoingsurgery for epilepsy, non-aneurysmal vascular deformities andischaemic stoke. Further evidence is required in all these groupsbefore the true efects of hypothermia can be determined in thesepopulations.A U T H O R S '   C O N C L U S I O N S

Implications for practiceAt present there is no evidence to support the use of hypothermiain patients undergoing craniotomy, although further high qualitystudies are required to determine its true eficacy and safety acrossdiferent neurosurgical populations.Implications for researchMore clinical trials are needed to establish the efectiveness ofhypothermia for cerebral protection during brain surgery beforedrawing any firm conclusions with regards to its potential in thesepatients. There is a need for more well designed and conductedclinical trials to detect clinically important diferences betweenhypothermia and normothermia. More information is also neededon the minimum efective target temperature, the time periodduring which cooling is appropriate, the best rewarming strategiesand potential complications.A C K N O W L E D G E M E N T SWe wish to acknowledge WRO Milani, PL Antibas and GF Prado, theauthors of the original review (Milani 2011) on this topic.
